body.p: XSpecToolsXSpec is a test framework XSpec on GitHub Test Framework for XSLT, XQuery, and Schematron. To test complex Schematron rules, for example, you define expected values for positive and negative test cases in the form of XML snippets in a test scenario.
In an XSpec file *.xspec, assert and not-assert methods Assert and Not-Assert are declared:
body.pre: <x:description xmlns:x="http://www.jenitennison.com/xslt/xspec" xslt-version="2.0" schematron="test.sch"> <x:scenario label="ALL"> <x:context href="test.xml"/> <x:expect-not-assert id="person-nachname-rule" location="//person[1]/nachname"/> <x:expect-assert id="person-nachname-rule" location="//person[2]/nachname"/> </x:scenario> </x:description>
body.p: In general, an assert means that the mapping between the actual value and the expected value of the test caseTest Case is positively fulfilled. With a not-assert, the opposite is the case. In the above example, two rules are sufficient to fully cover the test case.
When you want to test SchematronToolsSchematron rules using XSpec, you need to think around the corner a bit. Because these rules are also modeled using assert and not-assert.
body.pre: <sch:schema xmlns:sch="http://purl.oclc.org/dsdl/schematron" xmlns:xsl="http://www.w3.org/1999/XSL/Transform" queryBinding="xslt2"> <sch:pattern id="main"> <sch:rule context="nachname"> <sch:assert id="person-nachname-rule" role="error" test="normalize-space(.)"> Der Nachname der Person mit ID: <sch:value-of select="@id"/> fehlt! </sch:assert> </sch:rule> </sch:pattern> </sch:schema>
body.p: In the Schematron rule, it is asserted that every person has a last name.
If a person has no last name, then the error report is written to the Schematron result file. This file is then evaluated by XSpec.

note.p: When an error occurs (= the nachname field is empty), the assert rule fires in XSpec! This is the inverse logic compared to the Schematron rules.

body.p: As a mnemonic, you can equate an assert in the XSpec file with Appear and a not-assert with Not-Appear. Tips and TricksAppear and Not-Appear
An assert thus ensures that an error report for the test case exists in the Schematron result file. A not-assert ensures that no error report exists.
As you can easily imagine, assert rules in this case are easy to find - you just have to let the Schematron test rules point to nothing. Everything is green and everything is fine - or so it appears.
